question_cards.question_description_en: Based on our current understanding of physics, our technological needs, and our biological limitations, it is unlikely we will ever travel at the speed of light.

question_cards.original_question: What is quantum uncertainty and why is it important?
question_cards.core_question: What is the Heisenberg Uncertainty Principle (quantum uncertainty) and why is it fundamental to quantum mechanics and important for quantum computing?
sub_questions: What is the mathematical formulation of the Heisenberg Uncertainty Principle?
How does the uncertainty principle fundamentally limit simultaneous measurement of conjugate variables?
Why is quantum uncertainty not merely a limitation of measurement instruments?
How does quantum uncertainty enable and constrain quantum superposition in quantum computing?
What role does uncertainty play in the collapse of superpositions due to measurement or environmental interaction?

excluded: other uncertainty relations (e.g., energy-time) unless directly relevant
interpretations of quantum mechanics beyond standard formalism

search_keywords: 量子计算 不确定性

checkpoints: Can the mathematical inequality ΔxΔp ≥ ħ/2 be derived from quantum mechanical postulates?
Can experimental demonstrations (e.g., single-slit diffraction, which-path experiments) confirm the trade-off between position and momentum knowledge?
Can the principle be distinguished from classical measurement disturbance?
Can the role of uncertainty in protecting superposition states against certain types of noise be explained in a quantum computing context?
Can the collapse of superposition upon measurement be linked to the uncertainty principle?

assumptions.point: The term 'quantum uncertainty' in the question is interpreted as the Heisenberg Uncertainty Principle, as clarified by the background description.
assumptions.default_choice: Yes, it refers to the Heisenberg Uncertainty Principle.

assumptions.point: The importance of quantum uncertainty is primarily considered in the context of quantum computing, as emphasized in the background, but may also include foundational significance.
assumptions.default_choice: Importance covers both foundational quantum mechanics and quantum computing applications.

question_cards.question_description_en: The Heisenberg Uncertainty Principle is a key principle of quantum mechanics and states that we cannot measure an object’s velocity and location at the same time. “The more accurately you measure an object’s position, the more inherently inaccurate your knowledge of its momentum becomes,” writes astronomer and science journalist Ethan Siegel. “It isn’t just a failure of our instrumentation; that uncertainty is fundamental to the Universe.” Uncertainty serves an important function in quantum computing, which relies on electron superposition to store information. But quantum superpositions are tricky things. They can collapse (in quantum speak) when you try to measure them or even through a common interaction with the environment, such as encountering a random pulse of electromagnetic radiation.

question_cards.original_question: What are the smallest building blocks of matter?
question_cards.core_question: What are the fundamental, indivisible particles that serve as the smallest building blocks of all matter, according to the Standard Model of particle physics?

context.time_scale: from ~10^-12 seconds after the Big Bang to present

sub_questions: What are the six types of quarks and their properties?
What are the six types of leptons and their properties?
How do gauge bosons mediate fundamental forces?
Is there experimental evidence that quarks and leptons are indivisible?
Could there be substructure beyond the Standard Model?

included: Standard Model elementary particles (quarks, leptons, gauge bosons, Higgs boson)
excluded: hypothetical particles beyond the Standard Model (e.g., preons, strings) unless evidence emerges

checkpoints: High-energy scattering experiments (e.g., at LHC) show no internal structure of quarks and leptons down to ~10^-19 m.
No statistically significant excess of events indicating excited states or compositeness of quarks/leptons.
Precision measurements of anomalous magnetic moments and coupling constants agree with point-like particle predictions.
Discovery of any sub-quark or sub-lepton structure would falsify the claim that they are the smallest building blocks.

assumptions.point: The question is interpreted within the established framework of the Standard Model, assuming no substructure of quarks and leptons.
assumptions.default_choice: Accept Standard Model as current best answer; acknowledge open questions about preons or string theory.

question_cards.question_description_en: The objects we observe and interact with every day are made of atoms, each approximately 10–10 meters in size. But the question of what matter is made of doesn’t end there. Inside the cavernous space of an atom are electrons orbiting a nucleus consisting of protons and neutrons. In fact, the majority of the mass (over 99%) of an atom is found inside the nucleus. The riddle concerning the basic building blocks of matter originates inside protons and neutrons, and the solution forms the basis of particle physics, a well tested theory known as the Standard Model. The Standard Model establishes that there are fundamental particles that cannot be subdivided further. All matter in the universe consists of fermions, of which there are two types: quarks and leptons. There are six types of quarks that exist in pairs, or “generations”: up and down quarks, top and bottom quarks, and charm and strange quarks. Leptons also have multiple types of generations: electrons and the electron neutrinos, muons and muon neutrinos, and taus and tau neutrinos. “The electron, the muon and the tau all have an electric charge and a sizeable mass, whereas the neutrinos are electrically neutral and have very little mass,” notes the European Organization for Nuclear Research (CERN), home of the world’s largest and most powerful particle accelerator. While much about these fundamental particles remains an enigma, there is quite a bit we do know about them. Quarks first appeared in the nascent universe approximately 10–12 seconds after the Big Bang. And with the advent of advanced particle accelerators such as cyclotrons and sensor techniques and technology, we have been able to observe quarks right here on Earth. There are other fundamental particles, known as bosons, which essentially serve as force carriers. Each force has its own corresponding boson; for example, the gluon is the boson that carries the strong force. While two fermions cannot occupy the same quantum state, there is no limit to the number of bosons that can be put into the same quantum state.

question_cards.original_question: Why does time seem to flow in only one direction?
question_cards.core_question: Why does time exhibit a unidirectional arrow, as evidenced by the second law of thermodynamics and our perception of past and future, and what is the physical origin of this arrow in the context of cosmology?

context.time_scale: cosmological (from Big Bang to heat death)
conditions: initial low-entropy state at the Big Bang
current non-equilibrium state of the universe
future equilibrium (heat death) where arrow of time vanishes

sub_questions: Why does the second law of thermodynamics mandate entropy increase in isolated systems?
What set the initial low-entropy condition of the universe at the Big Bang?
How does the thermodynamic arrow of time align with the psychological arrow of time (our perception of past and future)?
Would the arrow of time truly disappear at thermodynamic equilibrium, and what would that imply for causality?
Are there other arrows of time (e.g., cosmological, radiative) that coincide with or differ from the thermodynamic arrow?

included: psychological arrow of time in relation to entropy
excluded: quantum arrow of time (e.g., wave function collapse)
biological or evolutionary arrows of time not directly tied to thermodynamics

checkpoints: Observational confirmation of a low-entropy early universe (e.g., cosmic microwave background uniformity).
Experimental verification that entropy never decreases in isolated systems.
Demonstration that in a system at maximum entropy, no macroscopic time asymmetry exists.
Theoretical models showing that memory and perception require entropy gradients aligned with the thermodynamic arrow.
Cosmological data consistent with an expanding universe that started in a special low-entropy state.

assumptions.point: The question assumes the thermodynamic arrow is the primary explanation for the perceived flow of time.
assumptions.default_choice: Focus on the thermodynamic arrow as the main candidate; other arrows are secondary.

assumptions.point: The background assumes the universe began in a low-entropy state and will reach equilibrium (heat death).
assumptions.default_choice: Accept the standard Big Bang model and future heat death scenario as the working framework.

question_cards.question_description_en: According to the second law of thermodynamics, isolated systems move from states of low entropy to high entropy—that is, from order to chaos. What we observe as time moving forward is actually an increase in entropy. Once a system achieves its fully disordered state (known as equilibrium), entropy is no longer increasing, and the arrow of time evaporates. In essence, we currently experience the forward movement of time because we’re not in equilibrium. The arrow of time is an interesting aspect of cosmology. The Big Bang accelerated all matter into a state of high entropy. As it continues its expansion, eventually all its components—stars, black holes, and galaxies—will burn out, leading to equilibrium and the disappearance of time’s arrow.

question_cards.original_question: Are there any particles that behave oppositely to the properties or states of photons?
question_cards.core_question: Do any particles exist that exhibit properties or states exactly opposite to those of a photon (massless, chargeless, spin-1, boson, speed c, electromagnetic interaction), considering that the antiphoton is identical to the photon?
question_cards.research_object: hypothetical particle with properties opposite to the photon
sub_questions: What are the defining properties of a photon?
What would constitute 'opposite' for each property?
Are there any known particles in the Standard Model that satisfy all opposite criteria?
Do theoretical extensions (e.g., supersymmetry, dark sector) predict such a particle?
How could the existence of such a particle be experimentally tested or falsified?
search_keywords: particle with mass and charge opposite to photon

checkpoints: Define a complete set of photon properties and their logical opposites.
Check all known particles in the Standard Model against this opposite profile; if any match, answer is yes.
If no known particle matches, examine well-motivated BSM theories (e.g., SUSY, extra dimensions) for candidates.
Design an experiment to detect a particle with opposite properties (e.g., massive, charged, fermionic) and set exclusion limits.
If no candidate exists theoretically and experimental searches yield null results, the answer is likely 'no'.

assumptions.point: Meaning of 'behave oppositely' – does it require all properties to be inverted, or only some?
assumptions.default_choice: All fundamental properties (mass, charge, spin, statistics, interaction) are inverted relative to the photon.

assumptions.point: Scope of particle types – only elementary particles, or also composite?
assumptions.default_choice: Elementary particles only, as composite particles can have mixed properties.

assumptions.point: Is the question about real particles or also virtual/off-shell states?
assumptions.default_choice: Real, asymptotic particles.

question_cards.question_description_en: An antiphoton is the same as a photon. Both are massless particles made of energy with no charge.

question_cards.original_question: Will Bose–Einstein condensates be widely used in the future?
question_cards.core_question: Will Bose–Einstein condensates be widely adopted in practical technologies (e.g., atom lasers, quantum computing, precision sensing) within the next 20–50 years?

key_variables.name: Cost and scalability of ultracold systems
Investment and research funding in BEC applications

sub_questions: What are the most promising near-term practical applications of BEC?
What technical barriers currently prevent BEC from being used outside specialized labs?
How do BEC-based devices (e.g., atom lasers, quantum simulators) compare to existing technologies in performance, cost, and reliability?
What is the projected timeline for commercialization of BEC technologies according to expert roadmaps?
Are there any niche markets where BEC could become dominant even without widespread adoption?

included: practical devices and systems based on BEC
quantum computing, sensing, and metrology with BEC
excluded: purely fundamental studies of BEC without application potential
other ultracold matter platforms (e.g., degenerate Fermi gases) unless directly compared to BEC

search_keywords: 玻色-爱因斯坦凝聚 应用 前景
超冷原子 技术 路线图

checkpoints: By 2040, are there commercially available products or services whose core functionality relies on BEC?
Do BEC-based quantum computers or simulators demonstrate a clear quantum advantage over classical or other quantum platforms in at least one practical task?
Has the number of patents or startups related to BEC applications grown exponentially over a decade?
Are major technology companies investing in BEC R&D for product development?
Do industry consortia or standards bodies include BEC-based technologies in their roadmaps?

assumptions.point: Definition of 'widely used'
assumptions.default_choice: Adoption beyond fundamental research labs, into at least one commercial or industrial sector with multiple products or services.

assumptions.point: Time horizon of 'future'
assumptions.default_choice: 20–50 years from present (2025).

assumptions.point: Scope of 'use'
assumptions.default_choice: Practical technology applications, not purely scientific research tools.

assumptions.point: What counts as a BEC-based technology
assumptions.default_choice: A device or system where the BEC phase is essential to its operation, not just a byproduct.

question_cards.question_description_en: A Bose–Einstein condensate is an exotic state of matter consisting of a dense cloud of ultracold atoms. Potential applications range from atom lasers to quantum computing.

question_cards.original_question: What is the maximum speed to which we can accelerate a particle?
question_cards.core_question: What is the maximum achievable speed for a massive particle in a human-made particle accelerator, considering both current technology and foreseeable future advances, as well as fundamental physical constraints?
question_cards.research_object: massive particle (e.g., proton, electron) in an artificial accelerator
sub_questions: What is the theoretical upper bound on the speed of a massive particle according to special relativity?
What is the highest speed achieved by protons in the LHC (in terms of v/c)?
What are the practical energy limits of current and planned circular colliders (e.g., FCC-hh) and how do they translate to particle speed?
Can linear accelerators or advanced acceleration schemes (e.g., plasma wakefield) overcome the synchrotron radiation limit and reach higher effective speeds?
Are there fundamental physical limits (e.g., Schwinger limit, quantum effects) that set an absolute maximum energy and thus speed for any accelerator?

included: both circular and linear accelerator designs
excluded: natural acceleration mechanisms (cosmic rays, astrophysical jets)

checkpoints: Confirm that special relativity forbids any massive particle from reaching exactly c, setting an asymptotic limit.
Determine the current record proton speed at LHC (e.g., 0.999999991c at 6.5 TeV) and corresponding Lorentz factor.
Evaluate the maximum beam energy achievable by next-generation circular colliders (FCC-hh ~100 TeV) and compute the resulting speed.
Assess whether linear colliders or advanced acceleration techniques can surpass the energy reach of circular machines and thus yield higher speeds.
Identify any fundamental physical thresholds (e.g., quantum electrodynamics breakdown, Unruh effect) that would impose an absolute ceiling on acceleration.

assumptions.point: The term 'particle' is interpreted as a massive particle, since massless particles always travel at c.
assumptions.default_choice: massive particle

assumptions.point: The question is about human-made accelerators, not natural cosmic accelerators.
assumptions.default_choice: artificial accelerators only

assumptions.point: The 'maximum speed' refers to the instantaneous speed of the particle, not an average over a macroscopic distance.
assumptions.default_choice: instantaneous speed

assumptions.point: The scope includes foreseeable future technologies (next 50-100 years), not speculative far-future concepts.
assumptions.default_choice: near-future technology

question_cards.question_description_en: The Large Hadron Collider at CERN is the most powerful particle accelerator in the world. It can accelerate particles close to the speed of light.

question_cards.original_question: Can we accurately simulate the macro- and microworld?
question_cards.core_question: Can we achieve accurate simulation of both macroscopic and microscopic physical systems, given that macroscopic simulation is straightforward classically while microscopic simulation requires powerful quantum computers?
question_cards.research_object: simulation of physical systems at macro and micro scales
sub_questions: What is the current accuracy of macroscopic simulations?
Is a quantum computer necessary for accurate microscopic simulation?
What level of quantum computing capability is required to accurately simulate the microworld?
How is 'accurate simulation' defined for macro and micro scales?

checkpoints: Demonstration of a quantum computer simulating a microphysical system (e.g., molecular dynamics) with fidelity exceeding a predefined threshold
Comparison of classical simulation error for the same system
Establishment of accuracy benchmarks for macro and micro simulations

assumptions.point: Definition of 'accurately' in simulation
assumptions.default_choice: Reproducing observable phenomena within scientifically acceptable error bounds

assumptions.point: Macroscopic simulation is already sufficiently accurate
assumptions.default_choice: Classical simulations of the macroworld are accurate enough for most practical purposes

question_cards.question_description_en: While we can typically simulate the macroworld in a straightforward manner, doing so at the microlevel requires rigorous and powerful quantum computers. We are getting closer.

question_cards.original_question: Can humans make intense lasers with incoherence comparable to sunlight?
question_cards.core_question: Is it feasible to generate high-power lasers with incoherence properties (broad frequency spectrum, random polarization) comparable to sunlight?
question_cards.research_object: High-power lasers with sunlight-like incoherence (broadband, randomly polarized)

conditions: High-power laser regime for inertial confinement fusion

sub_questions: What are the quantitative incoherence parameters of sunlight (bandwidth, coherence length, polarization randomness)?
What is the current state-of-the-art in high-power laser incoherence?
What technologies can increase laser incoherence (e.g., broadband seeding, random phase plates, nonlinear amplification)?
Can these technologies maintain high power while achieving sunlight-level incoherence?
How effectively would such a laser suppress laser-plasma instabilities?
search_keywords: 激光等离子体不稳定性

checkpoints: Experimental demonstration of a high-power laser (>1 TW) with simultaneous broad bandwidth (>100 nm) and random polarization.
Measured coherence length of the laser comparable to sunlight (~1 μm).
Observation of significant suppression of stimulated Raman scattering or other instabilities in laser-plasma experiments using this laser.

assumptions.point: Threshold for 'intense lasers'
assumptions.default_choice: Petawatt-class or high-power lasers used for inertial confinement fusion research

assumptions.point: Definition of 'incoherence comparable to sunlight'
assumptions.default_choice: Broad visible spectrum and random polarization, with coherence length on the order of 1 μm

question_cards.question_description_en: Intense lasers with properties resembling sunlight, such as a broad frequency spectrum and random polarization, have significant advantages in reducing resonant excitation of certain instabilities in laser– plasma interactions. Such instabilities often lead to reduced laser-energy absorption in plasma and produce harmful energetic electrons that become one of the most significant challenges to the realization of laser-driven inertial confinement fusion. Scientists are developing technologies to generate broadband lasers, and intense lasers have been planned for the next-generation of high-power lasers for fusion research. Inertial confinement fusion is well recognized as one of the most promising prospects for fusion energy, a useful and potentially sustainable energy source. If we can generate high-power lasers with properties aligned with sunlight, we may eventually realize controllable fusion ignition and fusion energy on Earth.

question_cards.original_question: Is quantum many-body entanglement more fundamental than quantum fields?
question_cards.core_question: Is quantum many-body entanglement more fundamental than quantum fields, in the sense that quantum field theory and spacetime can be derived from entanglement, and that entanglement provides a more unified framework for physics?
question_cards.research_object: The comparative fundamentality of quantum many-body entanglement versus quantum fields
sub_questions: Can quantum field theory be rigorously derived from a framework based solely on quantum many-body entanglement?
Can spacetime geometry and general relativity emerge from patterns of many-body entanglement?
Does entanglement offer a more unified description of quantum matter and quantum information than quantum fields?
What criteria should be used to judge whether one physical concept is more fundamental than another?
Are there phenomena that can be explained by entanglement but not by quantum fields, or vice versa?

included: theoretical derivations of QFT from entanglement
unification of quantum information and quantum matter
excluded: purely experimental studies without theoretical fundamentality implications

checkpoints: Existence of a rigorous formal derivation of quantum field theory (or its core features) from a many-body entanglement framework.
Demonstration that spacetime metric and Einstein's equations emerge from entanglement structure in a well-defined model.
Proof that entanglement-based framework can incorporate general relativity while quantum field theory alone cannot.
Identification of physical phenomena uniquely predicted or explained by the entanglement-first view that are inaccessible to standard QFT.
Satisfaction of a predefined set of criteria for fundamentality (e.g., logical priority, ontological parsimony, unifying power) by the entanglement framework over quantum fields.

assumptions.point: The meaning of 'more fundamental' is ambiguous; it could refer to ontological priority, logical derivability, or greater unifying power.
assumptions.default_choice: Fundamentality is defined as the ability to derive the other concept and to provide a more unified framework for known physics.

assumptions.point: The term 'quantum many-body entanglement' might refer to specific types (e.g., long-range entanglement, topological entanglement) or generic multipartite entanglement.
assumptions.default_choice: It refers to generic patterns of entanglement in many-body systems, including area-law and beyond-area-law states.

assumptions.point: The term 'quantum fields' could mean the fundamental entities of QFT or the mathematical field operators.
assumptions.default_choice: It refers to quantum fields as the fundamental dynamical entities in QFT.

question_cards.question_description_en: Quantum entanglement involves how the behavior of two particles impacts each other, even when they are not physically close—like twins separated at birth. It has been used to explain many important physical phenomena, such as superconductivity, for example, and can help us develop a framework to better understand the physical world, and possibly even a new direction to unify different physics theories. Scientists are endeavoring to construct theories based on quantum entanglement from which quantum field theory can be derived. A central theme is to understand how space-time can emerge from many body quantum entanglement, and how general relativity can be incorporated into the physical picture of unified quantum information and quantum matter. Ultimately, quantum entanglement can offer a new way of thinking to predict physical phenomena.

question_cards.original_question: Can we make a real, human-size invisibility cloak?
question_cards.core_question: Is it physically possible to construct a real, human-sized invisibility cloak that works in the visible spectrum, given fundamental bounds on cloaking (bandwidth, scattering suppression, object size) and limitations from causality and stability?
sub_questions: What are the exact fundamental bounds on cloaking for a human-sized object in the visible spectrum?
Can any known or hypothetical material satisfy the required constitutive parameters while respecting causality and stability?
Is there a theoretical loophole (e.g., active cloaking, non-Euclidean transformation optics) that circumvents these bounds?
What is the maximum achievable scattering reduction for a human-sized cloak under realistic material constraints?

excluded: approximate or partial cloaking that does not aim for full invisibility

checkpoints: A rigorous theoretical proof that any passive cloak of human size in the visible spectrum must violate either causality or stability, or exceed available material properties.
Derivation of a quantitative bound showing that scattering suppression below a detectable threshold is impossible for the given size and bandwidth.
Absence of any experimental demonstration of a human-size visible-light cloak with full invisibility after reasonable efforts, consistent with the theoretical bounds.

assumptions.point: The 'real' invisibility cloak is assumed to operate in the visible spectrum (400–700 nm) and render a human completely undetectable to the naked eye under white-light illumination.
assumptions.default_choice: Visible spectrum, full invisibility

assumptions.point: The cloak is assumed to be passive, i.e., no external energy input or active control.
assumptions.default_choice: Passive cloak

assumptions.point: The fundamental bounds mentioned refer to the trade-off between bandwidth, scattering reduction, and object size, as well as constraints from Kramers-Kronig relations and passivity.
assumptions.default_choice: Standard interpretation from transformation optics and metamaterial literature

question_cards.question_description_en: Not likely. We will be prevented by fundamental bounds on cloaking (i.e., bandwidth, level of scattering suppression, and object size), combined with fundamental limitations stemming from causality and stability.

question_cards.original_question: Will there ever be a “theory of everything”?
question_cards.core_question: Will a complete theory of everything, unifying general relativity and quantum field theory and explaining all known physical phenomena (including dark matter and dark energy), ever be formulated and empirically validated?
question_cards.research_object: Theory of Everything (ToE): a single, consistent framework unifying all fundamental forces and particles

conditions: must reconcile quantum mechanics and general relativity
must incorporate dark matter and dark energy
must reproduce Standard Model at low energies
must be mathematically consistent and empirically testable

key_variables.name: candidate theoretical framework
key_variables.category: approach (e.g., string theory, loop quantum gravity)

sub_questions: Can all fundamental forces be unified at high energies?
Is a consistent quantum theory of gravity possible?
Can dark matter and dark energy be explained within a unified framework?
Are candidate theories like string theory empirically testable?
Are there fundamental limits (logical, mathematical, or observational) that prevent a ToE?

included: all fundamental interactions (gravitational, electromagnetic, strong, weak)
excluded: purely philosophical arguments without physical constraints

checkpoints: A mathematically self-consistent theory is formulated that includes general relativity and the Standard Model as low-energy limits.
The theory makes novel, testable predictions (e.g., new particles, corrections to known processes) that are confirmed by experiment or observation.
The theory provides a consistent account of dark matter and dark energy.
No logical inconsistency or experimental contradiction is found after extensive scrutiny.
Alternatively, a rigorous proof demonstrates that such a unification is impossible in principle (e.g., due to incompatibility of quantum mechanics and gravity).

assumptions.point: The term 'theory of everything' refers to a physical theory within the scope of natural science, not a metaphysical or philosophical system.
assumptions.default_choice: Assume a scientific, empirically testable theory.

assumptions.point: Human scientific progress will continue, and we will be able to probe the necessary energy scales or find indirect evidence.
assumptions.default_choice: Assume continued progress in theoretical and experimental physics.

question_cards.question_description_en: For decades, physicists have been tirelessly working towards a theory of everything: one theory and set of simple, fundamental principles that can explain all of the physical phenomena in the universe. Einstein’s Physics theory of relativity and the discovery of quantum mechanics served as the impetus for this endeavor, and although we have made progress, the prospect of such a theory breaks down when comparing quantum-level systems with macro systems. While some scientists view a grand unified theory as fantasy, there is still a desire to pursue it, and ever increasingly, physicists are drawing from innovative sources for inspiration—and a potential framework. When Einstein began his work in the 1920s, he asserted there was a bridge between the need to unify gravity and electromagnetism (the two fundamental forces that were known at the time), and the challenges of quantum mechanics. There has been movement towards this goal. “In the 1960s, physicists demonstrated that electromagnetism and the weak nuclear force were actually a single phenomenon, and the Higgs Boson is the reason why the two forces seem so different,” says physicist Don Lincoln of Fermilab in an educational video from the U.S. Department of Energy. “Naturally, scientists wonder if the seemingly independent remaining forces…might actually be different manifestations of a simpler and more inclusive force.” Analysis of the fundamental forces, in particular a comparison of force strength as it relates to energy, prompts more discussion. In what Lincoln calls “a most provocative coincidence,” the strength of three forces (strong, weak, and electromagnetic) become the same at the high energy of 1015 electronvolts. The Standard Model of particle physics has been referred to as a “theory of almost everything,” because it still does not reconcile certain aspects of the universe, such as the nature of dark matter and dark energy. While some physicists conjecture that one of the best platforms for a theory of everything is string theory, in which particles are one-dimensional “strings” vibrating in 11 dimensions, the debate rages on. As Einstein said in his Nobel lecture, “The intellect seeking after an integrated theory cannot rest content with the assumption that there exist two distinct fields totally independent of each other by their nature.”

question_cards.original_question: What is the ultimate statistical invariance of turbulence?
question_cards.core_question: What is the ultimate statistical invariance (e.g., scaling symmetry, invariant measure, or universal probability distribution) that characterizes the turbulent energy cascade across scales in the limit of infinite Reynolds number?
sub_questions: What are the known statistical invariances in turbulence (e.g., Kolmogorov's 1941 scaling)?
How do intermittency corrections modify or break these invariances?
Is there a universal invariant measure or symmetry group underlying the turbulent cascade?
Can the ultimate invariance be derived from the Navier-Stokes equations or from renormalization group arguments?
What are the implications of such an invariance for other complex nonlinear systems?
search_keywords: 湍流 终极 标度律

checkpoints: Existence of a universal scaling function or symmetry group that collapses all velocity increment statistics across Reynolds numbers.
Derivation from first principles (Navier-Stokes equations) or rigorous renormalization group analysis predicting a specific invariant.
Experimental or numerical validation of the predicted invariant across different flows and Reynolds numbers.
Falsification: demonstration that no single invariant can describe all turbulent statistics due to intrinsic intermittency or boundary effects.

assumptions.point: The term 'ultimate' may refer to the asymptotic limit of infinite Reynolds number or to the most fundamental level of description.
assumptions.default_choice: We assume 'ultimate' means the asymptotic, scale-invariant limit at infinite Reynolds number, seeking a universal statistical law.

assumptions.point: The question presupposes that such an invariance exists and is discoverable.
assumptions.default_choice: We assume the existence of an ultimate statistical invariance is an open but meaningful hypothesis.

question_cards.question_description_en: To better understand turbulence, one of the models of general complex nonlinear systems, scientists are collaborating across the fields of math, physics, computer science, and engineering.

question_cards.original_question: What is dark matter?
question_cards.core_question: What is the fundamental nature and composition of dark matter, given that it constitutes approximately 27% of the universe, does not consist of ordinary baryonic matter, antimatter, or large black holes, and may be composed of new elementary particles (e.g., WIMPs, axions, sterile neutrinos) or massive compact halo objects (MACHOs)?

conditions: accounts for ~27% of the universe's mass-energy
does not emit, absorb, or reflect light
not composed of ordinary baryonic matter
candidate explanations include MACHOs and new elementary particles

sub_questions: What are the viable particle candidates for dark matter and their predicted properties?
How can dark matter be detected directly, indirectly, or produced in colliders?
What astrophysical observations can distinguish between particle dark matter and MACHOs?
How does dark matter influence the formation and evolution of cosmic structures?
What is the relationship between dark matter and dark energy?

included: particle physics candidates (WIMPs, axions, sterile neutrinos, etc.)
observational signatures (gravitational lensing, galaxy rotation curves, CMB)
theoretical models beyond the Standard Model

search_keywords: 弱相互作用大质量粒子

checkpoints: Direct detection experiments (e.g., XENON, LUX-ZEPLIN) observe a statistically significant excess of nuclear recoil events consistent with a dark matter particle.

Indirect detection experiments (e.g., Fermi-LAT, AMS-02) observe an excess of gamma rays, positrons, or antiprotons with spatial distribution matching dark matter halo profiles and no astrophysical background explanation.

Collider experiments (e.g., at the LHC) produce missing transverse energy signatures consistent with a new stable weakly interacting particle, with cross-sections and masses matching astrophysical constraints.
Astrophysical surveys rule out MACHOs as the dominant component via microlensing observations toward the Magellanic Clouds.
A theoretical model of a new particle is confirmed by multiple independent experimental approaches and explains all cosmological observations (CMB, structure formation, etc.).

assumptions.point: Dark matter is composed of one or more species of elementary particles not yet discovered, rather than macroscopic objects.
assumptions.default_choice: Assume particle nature is the leading hypothesis, but keep MACHOs as a viable alternative until ruled out.

assumptions.point: Dark matter interacts only gravitationally and possibly via weak force; no electromagnetic or strong interactions.
assumptions.default_choice: Assume standard WIMP-like weak-scale interaction, but consider other portals (e.g., axion-photon coupling).

question_cards.question_description_en: Approximately 95% of the constitution of the universe is unknown to us. That 95% seemingly has to account for the acceleration of the expansion of the universe, which has been proven thanks to data obtained by astronomers with modern instruments, starting with the Hubble Space Telescope. If the universe consisted only of observable matter, the accelerated expansion would not be happening—gravity would take hold. So, something unknown is causing this phenomenon. But what is it? We call this unknown dark matter and dark energy, which account for 25%–27% and 68% of the universe, respectively. And while we are literally in the dark about both of these, certain understandings have come to light in recent years, especially relating to dark matter. According to NASA, we have confirmed that dark matter does not consist of the same matter that makes up other universal objects. “It is not in the form of dark clouds of normal matter, matter made up of particles called baryons,” notes NASA’s science website. “We know this because we would be able to detect baryonic clouds by their absorption of radiation passing through them.” We know that dark matter does not emit light—in fact, observations have clarified that there is not enough visible matter to make up the 27%. We also know that dark matter cannot be antimatter. If this theory were true, we would detect unique gamma rays that occur when antimatter and matter collide and annihilate. Lastly, “we can rule out large galaxy-sized black holes on the basis of how many gravitational lenses we see. High concentrations of matter bend light passing near them from objects further away, but we do not see enough lensing events to suggest that such objects make up the required 25% dark matter contribution,” reports NASA. Astrophysicists are considering some interesting candidates. “Baryonic matter could still make up the dark matter if it were all tied up in brown dwarfs or in small, dense chunks of heavy elements. These possibilities are known as massive compact halo objects,” offers NASA. There is also strong evidence that dark matter consists of new types of elementary particles, such as weakly interacting massive particles (WIMPs), axions, or sterile neutrinos, among others. Physics

question_cards.original_question: What is the optimum hardware for quantum computers?
question_cards.core_question: What is the optimal hardware platform for building a practical quantum computer, considering current engineering and materials science constraints such as the need for low-temperature operation to maintain quantum coherence, integration with conventional control electronics, and scalability?

conditions: low-temperature operation required to prevent decoherence
integration with conventional hardware (user interface, operating systems)
use of qubits based on superposition and entanglement

key_variables.name: hardware platform type
key_variables.category: categorical variable representing technology (e.g., superconducting, trapped ion, silicon spin, topological)

sub_questions: What are the strengths and weaknesses of leading qubit technologies (superconducting, trapped ions, silicon spins, topological) in terms of coherence, gate fidelity, and scalability?
How does the requirement for low-temperature operation affect the choice of hardware platform and overall system design?
What materials science advances could mitigate decoherence and reduce cooling demands?
How can conventional control electronics and user interfaces be integrated with different quantum hardware platforms?
What metrics should be used to define 'optimum' in quantum hardware, and how can they be weighted for different applications?

excluded: quantum error correction codes independent of hardware
purely theoretical models without hardware relevance

search_keywords: 量子计算机 硬件 优化
超导量子比特 离子阱 硅量子比特 比较
superconducting qubit vs trapped ion vs silicon spin
硅基量子计算 材料

checkpoints: Define a set of performance metrics (e.g., quantum volume, gate fidelity, coherence time, scalability, operating temperature) and their relative importance.
Collect experimental data on these metrics for at least three major hardware platforms from peer-reviewed literature or benchmark databases.
Apply a multi-criteria decision analysis or optimization model to rank the platforms under realistic constraints (e.g., cooling power, fabrication yield).
Identify the platform that maximizes the chosen objective function; the answer is verifiable if independent groups can reproduce the ranking with the same data and criteria.

The hypothesis is falsified if a new hardware platform emerges that outperforms the identified optimum on the same metrics, or if the chosen metrics are shown to be inadequate for practical quantum computing.

assumptions.point: The term 'optimum' is ambiguous without specifying the optimization criteria and constraints.
assumptions.default_choice: Assume optimum means maximizing overall computational capability (e.g., quantum volume) while satisfying engineering constraints such as low-temperature operation, scalability, and integration with classical electronics.

assumptions.point: The background mentions low temperatures to prevent loss of quantum coherence, implying that room-temperature quantum computing is not currently feasible.
assumptions.default_choice: Assume that any candidate hardware must operate at cryogenic temperatures unless a room-temperature alternative is explicitly demonstrated.

assumptions.point: The question may be interpreted as asking for a single best hardware type or a combination of technologies.
assumptions.default_choice: Assume the question seeks a single optimal hardware platform, but hybrid approaches can be considered if they outperform pure platforms.

question_cards.question_description_en: Quantum computing relies on the changing phase of certain particles to encode information. Critical to a quantum computer is the superposition of states, i.e., the possibility of particles to be in two states at the same time, and the use of entanglement, where one particle’s behavior affects the others. The quest to design and build quantum hardware is a worldwide, multisector effort. A key goal is to integrate conventional hardware such as the user interface and operating systems with quantum mechanical systems such as π Josephson junctions to enable quantum bits (qubits) to be programmed and analyzed. Some materials research groups are considering silicon technologies. But there are still many challenges to overcome, particularly with the current need for low temperatures to prevent loss of quantum coherence. 28

question_cards.original_question: How can we develop manufacturing systems on Mars?
question_cards.core_question: How can we develop functional and economically viable manufacturing systems on Mars that leverage in-situ resources and operate reliably under extreme environmental constraints (e.g., low temperature, high radiation, microgravity, limited energy supply)?

context.spatial_scale: local habitat/outpost scale, potentially expandable to regional
conditions: limited initial energy and material supply from Earth
abundant regolith and potential subsurface water ice

key_concepts: energy generation and storage on Mars
closed-loop life support and manufacturing integration

key_variables.name: energy availability (power source type and capacity)
economic viability (cost per unit output vs. Earth import)

sub_questions: What are the most promising methods to process Martian regolith into structural materials with sufficient strength and durability?
How can 3D printing technologies be adapted to operate in Mars' low-pressure, low-temperature, and dusty environment?
What energy systems (nuclear, solar, chemical) can reliably power a Martian manufacturing base, and how should they be integrated?
How can manufacturing processes be automated or tele-operated to minimize human intervention given communication delays?
What supply chain and logistics models minimize Earth-dependence while maintaining economic feasibility?
How do microgravity and reduced atmospheric pressure affect key manufacturing processes (e.g., powder handling, melting, solidification, binding)?

included: manufacturing of structural components, tools, and spare parts on Mars
utilization of Martian regolith, atmosphere, and water ice as feedstock
additive manufacturing (3D printing) and other in-situ fabrication techniques
energy generation and storage solutions for manufacturing
economic trade-off analysis between local production and Earth resupply
excluded: purely biological or agricultural production (unless directly supporting manufacturing)
Earth-based manufacturing systems without adaptation to Mars
detailed human factors or habitat design not directly linked to manufacturing

checkpoints: A laboratory demonstration of manufacturing a structural component using Martian regolith simulant under simulated Mars environmental conditions (temperature, pressure, radiation).
A validated system-level model or prototype that integrates energy supply, feedstock processing, and fabrication units and shows sustained operation over a simulated Martian diurnal cycle.
A comparative life-cycle cost analysis showing that a proposed Mars manufacturing system is economically preferable to Earth resupply for a defined set of products over a 10-year mission.

Experimental evidence that key manufacturing processes (e.g., regolith sintering, polymer binding) produce materials with mechanical properties meeting predefined structural requirements (e.g., compressive strength > 20 MPa).
Successful tele-operation or autonomous execution of a multi-step manufacturing task with a communication delay of 4-24 minutes.

assumptions.point: A human presence on Mars is established with minimal initial infrastructure (habitat, power, basic life support).
assumptions.default_choice: Assume a pre-deployed robotic outpost with a nuclear or large solar power source and a basic habitat shell.

assumptions.point: The primary construction material will be regolith, possibly combined with imported binders or locally derived polymers.
assumptions.default_choice: Focus on regolith-based concrete alternatives (e.g., sulfur concrete, geopolymers) and sintered regolith.

assumptions.point: 3D printing is the leading candidate fabrication method, but other methods (e.g., pressing, casting) may also be viable.
assumptions.default_choice: Prioritize 3D printing but remain open to hybrid approaches.

assumptions.point: The question implies a long-term, sustainable colony, so economic viability includes amortization over decades.
assumptions.default_choice: Evaluate over a 20-year operational horizon.

question_cards.question_description_en: It is not too far-fetched to presume that one day human beings will leave Earth and emigrate to Mars. A stunning red orb with mountains and valleys not unlike those on our world, Mars is the planet in our solar system that is most similar to Earth. Exploratory missions, including China’s first Mars mission, Tianwen-1, which is currently in orbit, aim to reveal information that will be vital should we ever establish colonies there. But to take this step, we will need to develop functional and economically viable manufacturing systems that can operate in the unique ecosystem and climate limitations of Mars. The extreme environments of Mars challenge existing manufacturing theories and methods. Scientists and engineers must consider the many elements that would impact effective production there, including extreme temperature, solar radiation, and microgravity. We must also contend with questions about fuel and energy to power manufacturing and habitats on the Red Planet. This interdisciplinary quest requires scientists and engineers from seemingly disparate fields to collaborate across the globe. One key to Mars manufacturing will be utilizing the resources the planet has to offer. This is especially pertinent given the distance between Earth and Mars (225 million kilometers) and the prohibitive cost of shipping materials through space. Martian geology is a potential gold mine: Its regolith—a layer of loose, rocky material located on the planet’s surface—could be very useful as a prime construction material, once we develop the technical capabilities to bind it and build with it. 3D printing may be a game-changing technology in this respect, especially as we continue to advance our expertise in materials science, engineering, and chemistry, combined with the software and hardware innovations necessary for large-scale 3D printing.

question_cards.original_question: How can we break the current limit on energy-conversion efficiencies?
question_cards.core_question: How can the current limits on energy-conversion efficiencies (e.g., in photovoltaics, thermoelectrics, piezoelectrics, thermophotovoltaics) be overcome through novel materials, device architectures, or physical mechanisms?

conditions: factors limiting efficiency: photon wavelength, recombination, temperature, reflection

sub_questions: What are the fundamental physical mechanisms that impose the current efficiency limits in different energy-conversion systems?
Which material properties (e.g., bandgap tunability, carrier mobility, thermal conductivity) can be engineered to surpass these limits?
How can photon management strategies (e.g., up/down conversion, spectral splitting) reduce thermalization and transmission losses?
What novel device architectures (e.g., tandem, hot-carrier, intermediate-band) can exceed single-junction limits?
How can non-equilibrium or collective phenomena (e.g., exciton fission, ferroelectric polarization) be harnessed to boost efficiency?
What are the trade-offs between efficiency, stability, and cost when implementing these breakthrough approaches?
search_keywords: 突破能量转换效率极限
thermoelectric figure of merit ZT enhancement

checkpoints: Demonstration of a device with efficiency exceeding the single-junction Shockley-Queisser limit under standard test conditions.
Experimental realization of a thermoelectric material with ZT > 3 at room temperature.
Theoretical proposal validated by peer review that identifies a new physical mechanism allowing efficiency beyond current limits.
Reproducible measurement of a piezoelectric energy harvester with power conversion efficiency > 20% under realistic vibrations.
A thermophotovoltaic system achieving > 40% heat-to-electricity conversion efficiency.

assumptions.point: The question is interpreted as seeking general strategies across multiple energy-conversion technologies, not limited to photovoltaics.
assumptions.default_choice: Include thermoelectrics, piezoelectrics, and thermophotovoltaics as mentioned in the background.

assumptions.point: The 'current limit' refers to both practical commercial limits (15–20% for PV) and theoretical limits (e.g., Shockley-Queisser).
assumptions.default_choice: Consider both practical and theoretical limits.

assumptions.point: The question does not specify a particular time frame or technology readiness level.
assumptions.default_choice: Focus on near- to mid-term (5–20 years) research directions.

question_cards.question_description_en: Energy-conversion efficiency is the central metric in determining the success of an energy system. It simply takes the useful energy output (benefit) and divides it by the energy input (cost). In photovoltaic (PV), (solar) cells, the efficiency is the percentage of the solar energy shining on a PV device that is converted into usable electricity, as noted by the U.S. Department of Energy. “Improving this conversion efficiency is a key goal of research and helps make PV technologies cost-competitive with conventional sources of energy.” The energy efficiency of current commercially available cells is 15%–20%. Several factors contribute to the limit on energy efficiency, including the wavelength of photons, recombination, temperature, and reflection. Scientists in academia, industry, and government labs are experimenting with different materials and methods to push the current limit. Similar issues exist with other energy-conversion systems, such as thermoelectrics, piezoelectrics, and thermophotovoltaics.

question_cards.original_question: Is a future of only self-driving cars realistic?
question_cards.core_question: Is a transportation system composed exclusively of self-driving cars technically and sociotechnically feasible by 2050, given current constraints in sensor reliability, vehicle-to-everything (V2X) communication, road infrastructure adaptability, multi-agent traffic coordination, and public trust?
question_cards.research_object: full-scale deployment of Level 5 autonomous vehicles as the sole modality in urban and interurban transportation systems

conditions: existing road infrastructure with incremental upgrades
regulatory approval for zero fallback drivers

key_variables.name: V2X latency and packet loss rate
multi-vehicle coordination success rate in mixed-traffic simulations
public willingness to ride in AV-only networks (survey-based)
regulatory timeline for AV-only operation zones

sub_questions: What is the minimum required sensor false-negative rate for pedestrian detection at 60 km/h under rain/fog to achieve <1e-8 fatality/km in AV-only fleets?
Can V2X-enabled decentralized coordination eliminate phantom traffic jams and intersection collisions without centralized control in >10,000-vehicle/hour corridors?
At what infrastructure upgrade cost threshold (USD/lane-km) does AV-only deployment become economically dominated by hybrid (AV + legacy) or non-AV alternatives?
search_keywords: 全自动驾驶规模化可行性

checkpoints: Publication of peer-reviewed simulation studies demonstrating >99.999% collision-free coordination in stochastic high-density multi-agent scenarios (N≥10^6 agent-hours)
Empirical measurement of sensor failure rates meeting ISO 26262 ASIL-D requirements across ≥5 real-world weather/illumination edge cases
Three independent national regulatory agencies issuing binding timelines permitting AV-only zones on ≥10% of national highway mileage by 2035
Longitudinal survey data showing ≥75% stated willingness among representative adult populations to rely exclusively on AVs for daily commutes, sustained over ≥24 months

assumptions.point: ‘Only self-driving cars’ implies zero human-driven vehicles in the operational design domain (ODD), excluding emergency/maintenance exceptions
assumptions.default_choice: strict interpretation (zero human drivers)

assumptions.point: Feasibility assessment includes both technical functionality and socioeconomic adoption—not just engineering readiness
assumptions.default_choice: yes, sociotechnical feasibility required

assumptions.point: Time horizon ‘future’ is bounded to 2050 based on IPCC-aligned infrastructure planning cycles

question_cards.question_description_en: We cannot scale the use of self-driving cars until we solve underlying problems relating to their sensors, communication, infrastructure, and ability to respond to other vehicles—not to mention the public’s perception of autonomous vehicles.

question_cards.original_question: Is there an upper limit to computer processing speed?
question_cards.core_question: Is there a fundamental physical upper limit to computer processing speed, and if so, what physical constraints (e.g., energy, information density, signal propagation speed) determine this bound?
sub_questions: What physical limits apply to classical silicon-based computing speed?
Can quantum computing overcome the limits of classical computing?
What fundamental limits (e.g., from quantum mechanics, relativity) apply to any information processing?
Is there a universal upper bound on computation speed, such as Bremermann's limit, and is it achievable?
How do energy, information density, and signal transmission speed collectively determine an upper bound?

included: all types of computing devices (classical, quantum, optical, biological)
physical limits from thermodynamics, quantum mechanics, relativity
excluded: purely engineering or economic limits without physical basis
algorithmic complexity limits not related to hardware speed

checkpoints: Existence of a theoretical upper bound derived from fundamental physics (e.g., Bremermann's limit, Margolus-Levitin theorem)
Proof that no physical system can exceed this bound without violating established laws
Experimental verification that actual computing devices approach but do not exceed the bound
Consensus in physics community on the validity of the bound

assumptions.point: The term 'computer processing speed' refers to the raw physical operation rate at the hardware level, not algorithmic efficiency.
assumptions.default_choice: Hardware-level maximum physical operation rate.

assumptions.point: Whether to consider all possible physical substrates, including unknown physics, or only known physics.
assumptions.default_choice: Based on known physical laws (quantum mechanics, general relativity, thermodynamics).

assumptions.point: The upper limit is an absolute physical bound, not a temporary engineering barrier.
assumptions.default_choice: Seek fundamental physical upper bound.

question_cards.question_description_en: One of the most well-known principles of computing design and power is Moore’s Law, named after the cofounder of Intel Corporation, Gordon Moore. He predicted in the 1960s that that the number of transistors that could fit on a silicon chip would double every 2 years. This prediction is not a law of physics, but rather an empirical relationship related to production. However, Moore’s law will soon cease to be relevant, because the laws of physics will prevent further expansion of computing power. We can’t change the size of atoms, and thus won’t be able to pack more transistors into a computer beyond a certain point. But that’s where quantum computers can really shine. By their nature, quantum computers are not constrained by the size of a motherboard or microchip, which means the computer processing speed limits on silicon-based machines do not apply. The quantum superpositions of qubits allow for significantly more information to be stored and the power and speed of the machine itself to expand exponentially. Quantum computing also presents opportunities beyond qubits. Recently, scientists announced they had built a photon-based quantum computer that was able to achieve quantum supremacy: It outperformed a conventional supercomputer in a significant way, accomplishing in just 200 seconds a task called boson sampling that would have taken current supercomputers 2.5 billion years. Research in quantum computing is varied, plentiful, and well-funded, accelerated by federal governments and companies around the world investing billions of dollars. In 2017, a scientist at the U.S. National Institutes of Standards and Technology demonstrated the feasibility of a quantum computer that breaks previously suggested speed limits. He notes that his findings do not suggest the absence of limits on calculation speed, but rather that these limits derive from other aspects of physics than merely the availability of energy. “Energy considerations alone are not sufficient to obtain an upper bound on computational speed,” he writes. “Additional physical assumptions such as limits to information density and information transmission speed are necessary to obtain such a bound.” Information Science Information Science Can DNA act as an information storage medium? DNA, which stores huge amounts of organismal data at high densities with great stability, may one day be used to store other types of information. Can AI replace a doctor? There’s no doubt that artificial intelligence (AI) can improve our health care. With AI’s computing power, it could access monumentally more data and make connections more rapidly to diagnose and treat disease more efficiently. Imagine integrating patient and family histories, genetic profiles, and environmental stimuli to predict risk for illnesses or flare-ups even on a daily basis. AI has been shown to outperform physicians in cancer screenings and disease diagnoses. But it is not positioned to replace doctors— at least not yet. There’s still data bias. And currently, the involvement of a human doctor, who also relies on instinct, experience, and emotion to make medical decisions, cannot be replicated by AI. For now, rather than replace humans, some suggest that AI could improve the doctor– patient relationship and lead to better health care outcomes.

question_cards.original_question: Can topological quantum computing be realized?
question_cards.core_question: Can topological quantum computing be physically realized using non-abelian anyons in solid-state systems?

conditions: Existence of physical systems supporting non-abelian anyons
Ability to perform braiding operations on anyons

sub_questions: Do physical systems exist that host non-abelian anyons?
Can non-abelian anyons be created and manipulated in the laboratory?
Can topological qubits be initialized and read out reliably?
Can a universal set of topological quantum gates be implemented via braiding?
Can topological quantum computing scale to a practical number of qubits?

included: Non-abelian anyon-based approaches (e.g., Majorana zero modes, fractional quantum Hall states)
excluded: Non-topological quantum computing paradigms (e.g., superconducting qubits, trapped ions)

search_keywords: 拓扑量子计算 实现

checkpoints: Experimental observation of non-abelian braiding statistics in a physical system
Demonstration of a topologically protected qubit with coherence time significantly exceeding that of conventional qubits
Implementation of a universal set of quantum gates through braiding operations
Scalable fabrication of multiple topological qubits with low error rates

assumptions.point: The 'intermediate particles' mentioned in the background refer to non-abelian anyons.
assumptions.default_choice: Assume they are non-abelian anyons as required for topological quantum computing.

assumptions.point: The question 'can be realized' refers to physical realizability in principle, not necessarily near-term technological feasibility.
assumptions.default_choice: Interpret as asking whether there is any fundamental physical obstacle.

question_cards.question_description_en: Topological quantum computing involves exploitation of the phases of groups of particles through manipulation of their geometry. As naturally occurring quantum particles change position, their phase is represented either as a conventional manifold or mobius strip. Topological quantum computing is based on “intermediate particles” whose superposition produces other geometries, and thus has more flexibility for information processing. Traditional quantum computers are sensitive to external perturbations causing particle decoherence, but exploiting topologies may be able to get around some of their limitations, like the need for low temperatures or error correction schemes to overcome decoherence. Solid-state material investigations could enable a physical system that would support the “intermediate particles” needed.

question_cards.original_question: Can DNA act as an information storage medium?
question_cards.core_question: Can DNA function as a reliable and scalable medium for storing arbitrary digital information, leveraging its high density and stability?
question_cards.research_object: DNA molecule as a data storage medium
sub_questions: What encoding schemes can map digital data to DNA sequences?
What is the maximum achievable data density in DNA?
How does DNA stability vary with storage conditions?
What error rates occur during synthesis, storage, and sequencing?
Can random access and efficient retrieval be implemented?
Is DNA storage cost-competitive with conventional media?

included: in vitro storage of digital data

Synthesis of DNA strands representing the encoded data
Accurate decoding of the data after sequencing, with bit error rate below 10^-3
Demonstrated storage density exceeding 10^18 bytes per gram
Data integrity maintained after accelerated aging tests equivalent to years of storage

assumptions.default_choice: Assume digital data storage.

assumptions.point: The question does not specify requirements for random access or read/write speed.
assumptions.default_choice: Assume basic feasibility without strict performance constraints.

question_cards.question_description_en: DNA, which stores huge amounts of organismal data at high densities with great stability, may one day be used to store other types of information.

question_cards.original_question: Can AI replace a doctor?
question_cards.core_question: Can artificial intelligence systems completely replace human doctors in medical diagnosis, treatment, and patient interaction, given current technological limitations such as data bias and the irreplaceability of human intuition, experience, and emotion?
sub_questions: In which specific medical tasks can AI outperform human doctors?
Can AI handle medical decisions that require empathy, intuition, and emotional intelligence?
Can data bias in medical AI be sufficiently mitigated?
How would full AI replacement affect the doctor-patient relationship and patient satisfaction?
What are the ethical and regulatory barriers to AI replacing doctors?

AI systems can manage complex cases requiring emotional support and shared decision-making, as rated by patient satisfaction surveys.
Data bias is systematically reduced and AI performance remains consistent across demographic groups.
Long-term clinical trials show that AI-only care leads to equal or better health outcomes than human-doctor care.
Regulatory bodies approve fully autonomous AI doctors for unsupervised practice.

assumptions.default_choice: Focus only on AI replacing doctors.

assumptions.point: The term 'replace' could mean partial or full substitution. We assume full replacement of all doctor functions (diagnosis, treatment, patient interaction).
assumptions.default_choice: Full replacement scenario.

assumptions.point: The question does not specify a time horizon. We assume a near to mid-term future (10-30 years) based on current AI trends.
assumptions.default_choice: 10-30 year horizon.

question_cards.question_description_en: There’s no doubt that artificial intelligence (AI) can improve our health care. With AI’s computing power, it could access monumentally more data and make connections more rapidly to diagnose and treat disease more efficiently. Imagine integrating patient and family histories, genetic profiles, and environmental stimuli to predict risk for illnesses or flare-ups even on a daily basis. AI has been shown to outperform physicians in cancer screenings and disease diagnoses. But it is not positioned to replace doctors— at least not yet. There’s still data bias. And currently, the involvement of a human doctor, who also relies on instinct, experience, and emotion to make medical decisions, cannot be replicated by AI. For now, rather than replace humans, some suggest that AI could improve the doctor– patient relationship and lead to better health care outcomes. Can topological quantum computing be realized? Topological quantum computing involves exploitation of the phases of groups of particles through manipulation of their geometry. As naturally occurring quantum particles change position, their phase is represented either as a conventional manifold or mobius strip. Topological quantum computing is based on “intermediate particles” whose superposition produces other geometries, and thus has more flexibility for information processing. Traditional quantum computers are sensitive to external perturbations causing particle decoherence, but exploiting topologies may be able to get around some of their limitations, like the need for low temperatures or error correction schemes to overcome decoherence. Solid-state material investigations could enable a physical system that would support the “intermediate particles” needed.

question_cards.original_question: Where does consciousness lie?
question_cards.core_question: What is the physical or metaphysical substrate or location where consciousness arises or resides, considering both neural structures and proposed quantum processes in microtubules?
sub_questions: Is consciousness localized to specific brain regions or distributed across the brain?
Do microtubule quantum vibrations play a necessary role in generating consciousness?
Can consciousness exist independently of biological brains (e.g., as a fundamental feature of the universe)?
What is the minimal neural substrate sufficient for conscious experience?
How do perturbations to candidate substrates (e.g., anesthesia, TMS, microtubule disruption) affect consciousness?

included: biological consciousness in humans and animals
theories linking consciousness to spacetime geometry
excluded: artificial consciousness in machines (unless as comparative model)
purely philosophical definitions without empirical ties

search_keywords: 意识 位置 大脑 微管 量子意识 神经关联
consciousness location brain microtubules quantum consciousness neural correlates

checkpoints: If consciousness is entirely generated by a specific brain region, then selective lesion or inactivation of that region should abolish all conscious experience.
If microtubule quantum vibrations are necessary, then pharmacological or physical disruption of microtubules should eliminate consciousness while leaving other neural functions intact.

If consciousness is a fundamental feature of the universe, then brain damage might only alter the content of consciousness but not its existence, and conscious-like properties could be detected in non-biological systems.
High-resolution imaging and perturbation experiments should show a consistent, invariant mapping between the candidate substrate and the presence/absence of conscious report.

assumptions.point: The question assumes consciousness has a location or substrate that can be empirically investigated.
assumptions.default_choice: Assume consciousness is physically instantiated and localizable.

assumptions.point: The definition of consciousness excludes self-awareness, focusing on raw subjective experience.
assumptions.default_choice: Adopt the given definition from the background.

assumptions.point: The question may imply a single locus versus a distributed network; we assume both possibilities are open.
assumptions.default_choice: Keep the investigation open to both localized and distributed substrates.

question_cards.question_description_en: Consciousness could be described as the subjective experience of our minds. Indeed, consciousness is defined as everything a sentient being experiences. It does not necessarily imply self-awareness, and the two concepts should not be conflated. Naturally, the study of consciousness is a collaborative field that entices a diversity of scholars, including neuroscientists, philosophers, psychologists, and even electrical engineers. But despite a concerted effort, experts still disagree about the nature, mechanisms, and location of consciousness. In fact, the field of consciousness studies is considered rogue, controversial, and in some ways a minefield for mind scientists. Some scientists argue that consciousness is simply a chain of biochemical reactions, a dance of tangled neurons firing and signaling with every input and arousal. But others, including pioneering physicist Sir Roger Penrose, frequent collaborator of the late Stephen Hawking and 2020 Nobel laureate in physics, posit something else. Penrose and his colleague Stuart Hameroff, founder of the Center for Consciousness Studies at the University of Arizona, have been exploring consciousness for decades. Their controversial research proposes that “consciousness arises from quantum vibrations in protein polymers called microtubules inside the brain’s neurons, vibrations which interfere, ‘collapse’ and resonate across scale, control neuronal firings, generate consciousness, and connect ultimately to ‘deeper order’ ripples in spacetime geometry.” In this way, the team notes, “consciousness is more like music than computation.” Other scientists have conjectured that consciousness is a feature of the universe itself. A true understanding of the nature and mechanisms of consciousness will open the floodgates of knowledge to advance many fields that orbit neuroscience, including computer science and medicine. Our continued explorations of consciousness could buttress an understanding of the molecular mechanisms of complex brain disease and promote the development of brain-like intelligent algorithms. We have far to go and still need more elaborate high dimensional molecular data to simulate the brain’s dynamics, but fortunately, we are aided by modern techniques in high-resolution imaging technology and high-performance algorithms.

question_cards.original_question: What are the coding principles embedded in neuronal spike trains?
question_cards.core_question: What are the principles by which information about stimuli or internal states is represented in the temporal structure of individual neuronal spike trains?
question_cards.research_object: Coding principles in neuronal spike trains
sub_questions: What specific temporal features of spike trains (e.g., firing rate, precise spike timing, interspike intervals, bursts) carry information?
How can a stimulus be decoded or reconstructed from an observed spike train?
Do coding principles differ across brain regions, cell types, or behavioral states?
How do coding principles operate across different time scales, from rapid sensory events to slow modulations?
What are the theoretical limits on information transmission in spike trains?

excluded: local field potentials or subthreshold dynamics
molecular or genetic mechanisms of spike generation

checkpoints: A coding principle is identified if a systematic mapping between stimulus parameters and spike train statistics can be demonstrated, allowing stimulus reconstruction above chance level.
The principle is falsified if no consistent relationship between stimulus features and any spike train metric is found across controlled experiments.
A proposed coding model must predict neural responses to novel stimuli better than a null model based on firing rate alone.

assumptions.point: The question assumes that coding principles are embedded in spike trains and can be generalized across neurons or conditions.
assumptions.default_choice: Assume coding principles exist and are discoverable through analysis of spike timing patterns.

assumptions.point: The scope is limited to individual neurons, not population codes.
assumptions.default_choice: Focus on single-neuron spike trains as stated in the background.

question_cards.question_description_en: Spike trains—the readout of individual neurons firing—can be measured across different states in many species. How to connect (or “map”) a stimulus to a particular neuron’s response is not fully clarified, but is being intensively researched.

question_cards.original_question: Can human memory be stored, manipulated, and transplanted digitally?
question_cards.core_question: Can human episodic and semantic memories be digitally encoded, algorithmically manipulated, and functionally transplanted into a biological or artificial neural substrate while preserving content fidelity, temporal structure, affective valence, and behavioral relevance?
question_cards.research_object: Human memory engrams at the circuit- and synaptic-level

conditions: sufficient temporal resolution of neural recording

sub_questions: What is the minimal spatiotemporal neural signature sufficient to reconstruct a specific episodic memory with causal behavioral effect?
Can digitally synthesized neural activity patterns induce memory recall or behavioral conditioning indistinguishable from endogenous memory retrieval in humans?

included: human intracranial electrophysiology (e.g., SEEG), optogenetic/chemogenetic memory tagging in non-human primates, high-resolution connectomics of memory circuits, neuromorphic memory emulation

checkpoints: Demonstration of cross-subject memory transfer in non-human primates: donor memory trace recorded, digitally transformed, and implanted into recipient, resulting in statistically significant, cue-specific behavioral recall (p < 0.001, n ≥ 5 animals, blinded test).

Human pilot study showing that digitally reconstructed hippocampal-cortical firing sequences, delivered via closed-loop responsive neurostimulation, elicit verifiable, content-specific autobiographical recall (confirmed by independent narrative analysis + fMRI pattern similarity > 0.75 vs. natural recall).

Failure to achieve functional transplantation under controlled conditions (e.g., identical stimulation protocol fails when temporal jitter > 10 ms or spatial resolution degrades beyond 50 μm) falsifies feasibility at current technological limits.

assumptions.point: Human memory is sufficiently discretely encoded in stable, recordable, and reproducible neural activity patterns (i.e., no essential quantum or non-local physical mechanism prevents digital capture).
assumptions.default_choice: Assumed true for empirical tractability

assumptions.point: Behavioral and neural correlates of memory are sufficient proxies for subjective memory experience (i.e., no unmeasurable phenomenal property prevents functional validation).
assumptions.default_choice: Assumed true per operationalist neuroscience standards

question_cards.question_description_en: Science fiction movies depict a day when we can upload our memories to the cloud and then have them downloaded into some form of a computer—would this be a robot perhaps, or an artificial life form? Neuroscientists and cognitive science researchers don’t laugh at this prospect, but rather use it as inspiration to push the boundaries of knowledge and better understand how memory works and how it is stored in the bundles of neurons within our skulls. Memory is the way in which the brain retains information. Our brain can record all that we, as a conscious being, experience—the feelings something evokes; the smells, sounds, sights, and thoughts it engenders; and the actions that we take and observe.
